name: Seniors
description:
Seniors
Our services help seniors live in their communities as independently as possible. We work to make sure seniors and their families know what resources are available from the county, state and federal government.
We can help seniors apply for public assistance and navigate through home and community-based Medicaid programs, home care services and specialized housing. If you do not receive Medical Assistance, contact us to determine your eligibility for senior services and other programs the county offers.
To find out if you or someone in your care is eligible for Medical Assistance, visit the
Minnesota Department of Human Services website
.
To find out if you or someone in your care is eligible for other senior services, request a
MnCHOICES assessment online
or by calling
651-266-4500
.
Intake assessment
To apply for senior services, a social worker or public health nurse will complete an assessment to determine the best services to meet your long-term care needs and preferences. Ramsey County offers assessments through MnCHOICES, an initiative of the Minnesota Department of Health.
Learn more about the services seniors and people with disabilities can qualify for by completing a
MnCHOICES intake assessment
.
Community resources
Additional Assistance Programs
Medical Assistance
Elderly Waiver
Alternative Care Funds
Consumer Directed Community Supports
Contact us
Determine your eligibility for senior and disability services

name: Lake Owasso Residential Care
description:
Lake Owasso Residential Care
Lake Owasso services
Lake Owasso Residence (LOR) is a referral-only residential facility serving 64 people with developmental and intellectual disabilities such as down syndrome and autism. A referral to an Intermediate Care Facility for individuals with Developmental Disabilities (ICF/DD) in Minnesota is based on a screening process. The screening process determines if a person meets the eligibility criteria. A waiver case manager typically starts the referral process for the facility to review. Lake Owasso Residence is operated by Ramsey County, with the assistance of volunteers and community members, and offers special services and opportunities to adults with developmental and intellectual disabilities.
Lake Owasso Residence is in Shoreview on approximately 8 acres with about 300 feet of shoreline on Lake Owasso. There are 10 buildings on campus including an administration building, maintenance building and eight houses. Eight residents live in each of the eight houses for a total of 64 residents. Residential counselors work in the houses 24-hours, but do not live on campus.
Lake Owasso Residence is licensed by the Department of Human Services and the Minnesota Department of Health. Our individualized treatment and training plans are designed to increase independent living skills while strengthening relationships and community involvement. We encourage and promote person-centered decision making for programs and health care. Our services focus on creating adaptive skills allowing our residents to be as independent as possible in their work and communities. Our structure is unique in that a behavioral analyst works directly with our residential counselors to design and implement habilitative programs which promote adaptive behavior skills. It is rewarding for all when residents apply skills they have learned to be successful in everyday activities such as dressing, laundry, money management to go shopping. In addition, the onsite nursing department includes medical services for the treatment and support of ongoing and chronic health issues for our residents to maintain optimal health and functional ability.
Our well-trained residential counselors provide the highest quality programs and services to residents. The average tenure of an Lake Owasso Residence employee is 10 years. The team is comprised of seasoned professionals who know and understand disability services, state statutes and trends in services. Many employees participate in extra events with residents, such as coaching Special Olympics teams, fishing on the lake and leading sports activities. Employees are empowered to learn new ways to support residents and receive opportunities for training and development to enhance skills that will ultimately benefit residents. By investing in our employees, residents of Lake Owasso Residence and our whole community benefit.

Lake Owasso Residence is licensed by the Department of Human Services and the Minnesota Department of Health. Our individualized treatment and training plans are designed to increase independent living skills while strengthening relationships and community involvement. We encourage and promote person-centered decision making for programs and health care. Our services focus on creating adaptive skills allowing our residents to be as independent as possible in their work and communities. Our structure is unique in that a behavioral analyst works directly with our residential counselors to design and implement habilitative programs which promote adaptive behavior skills. It is rewarding for all when residents apply skills they have learned to be successful in everyday activities such as dressing, laundry, money management to go shopping. In addition, the onsite nursing department includes medical services for the treatment and support of ongoing and chronic health issues for our residents to maintain optimal health and functional ability.
Our well-trained residential counselors provide the highest quality programs and services to residents. The average tenure of an Lake Owasso Residence employee is 10 years. The team is comprised of seasoned professionals who know and understand disability services, state statutes and trends in services. Many employees participate in extra events with residents, such as coaching Special Olympics teams, fishing on the lake and leading sports activities. Employees are empowered to learn new ways to support residents and receive opportunities for training and development to enhance skills that will ultimately benefit residents. By investing in our employees, residents of Lake Owasso Residence and our whole community benefit.
What is our residential care like?
There are eight residential houses located on beautiful Lake Owasso in Shoreview, Minnesota. In these safe, comfortable group residences, adults with intellectual and developmental disabilities live together. Each person has his or her own bedroom and enjoys the support and supervision of caring employees 24 hours a day. Employees in each home provide supervision and training based on each person’s individual needs and plan. Volunteers provide companionship and group activities for residents and contribute financially to events that residents would not typically be able to attend, such as Twins baseball games or summer camping trips.
Our mission is to be a leading residential community where individuals with development and intellectual disabilities thrive, and are empowered through respect, person-centered decision making, and collaboration to achieve their individualized goals of stabilization and independence. We envision a future where each individual’s potential is fully realized through honest communication, active treatment,  and accountability, while delivering compassionate, high-quality services that uplift and transforms the lives of our residents and community.

name: Veterans Assistance
description:
Veterans Assistance
Ramsey County Veterans Services provides assistance to veterans, their dependents and survivors in applying for veterans’ benefits provided by the state of Minnesota and the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs. Approximately 23,000 veterans live in Ramsey County.
Veterans Services currently offers in-person appointments, along with virtual appointments by phone and video call. Appointments are encouraged. Walk-in clients are welcome but may not be immediately served. Call
651-266-2545
to schedule an appointment with a county Veterans Service Officer.
Contact Veterans Services
Visit Us
Walk-in and scheduled appointments are available at our office located at
90 Plato Blvd. W, Suite 210
.
Veterans Services staff are also available for walk-in appointments at the
Service Center - Maplewood
on Tuesdays, 1-4:30 p.m. and Thursdays, 10 a.m.-4:30 p.m.
Documents can be dropped off at
90 Plato Blvd. W, Suite 210
or faxed to 651-266-2546.
Get in touch with Veterans Services at
651-266-2545
or via our
Contact us form
.
Getting started
Our office helps veterans access VA health care benefits, file claims for VA compensation and VA pension, and apply for veterans' benefits through the state of Minnesota. To be eligible for benefits you must be a veteran, spouse, dependent, surviving spouse, qualifying child of a deceased veteran, active-duty military service member or a member of the Reserves or National Guard.
Using our services
Our County Veterans Service Officers (CVSOs) are certified and accredited to assist veterans in understanding the benefits they are eligible to receive, to assist in applying for those benefits and to work as advocates during the benefit process. We are available for appointments at our office Monday through Friday and do outreach through community events. To make an appointment call our main line at
651-266-2545
.
Veterans benefits
The Ramsey County Veterans Services office can assist veterans and their dependents with obtaining a variety of
federal, state and local benefits
.
Federal benefits include: disability, pension, health care, education, burial and home loans.
State benefits include: education, employment, health, burials, assistance and families.
Local benefits include: Disabled Veterans Property Tax Exemption, Minnesotans' Military Appreciation Fund and Beyond the Yellow Ribbon.

name: Financial Assistance
description:
Financial Assistance
Ramsey County helps determine your eligibility for financial assistance programs including:
emergency
,
cash
,
child care
,
food
and
health care
.
Emergency Assistance
is a short-term safety-net program designed to help residents address urgent, unexpected financial crises. An emergency is defined as circumstances that require immediate action which, if not resolved, will result in severe hardship and may include:
Past-due rent or mortgage
Damage deposit
Utilities when there is threat of disconnection. Utilities include electricity, heat, and water.
How to apply
Online
Apply for cash assistance, food assistance, emergency assistance and child care assistance programs online through
MNbenefits.mn.gov
. This is the fastest and easiest way to apply for these benefits.
Please submit only one application. Submitting multiple applications through different channels will delay the processing of your application.
Fax, in-person and email
Residents can also apply for benefits by:
Faxing applications to 651-266-3942.
Dropping applications off in a county
form drop-off & walk-in assistance site
.
Emailing applications to
[email protected]
.
Language services available
To better serve the needs of our non-English speaking residents, Ramsey County has added language options in the Financial Assistance call center greetings and call menus. Languages include Español, Hmoob, Soomaali and Karen.
Instructions
Dial
651-266-4444
.
Follow audio directions to select language option.
Greetings and menu options will be in the selected language.
Once connected with a call center agent, an interpreter will also support the call.
Check your benefit status
Review your benefit status by phone with EZ Info phone line or online using the new Case Status Tracker if you have you applied for, or are currently receiving SNAP (food), Cash, Child Care or Emergency Assistance.
Case Status Tracker
Access your eligibility, additional information needed to process your application and benefit status online using the new
Case Status Tracker
, visit
https://mnbenefits.mn.gov/status/pages/checkStatus
Use the primary applicant's case number, last name and birth date to access the information for the household.
Once assigned, a case number can be found in corresponding mail regarding your application.
It might take some time for your status to be available for verification purposes.
EZInfo
Ramsey County operates a 24/7 automated EZ Info phone line (
651-266-3800
) to provide information about financial assistance programs in English, Español, Hmoob, Soomaali and Karen.
Access your financial assistance eligibility, program and benefits status.
Request a form to report a change in your situation.
Related Resources
Operational Statistics Dashboard
Contact us
Financial Assistance Services
[email protected]
651-266-4444
Contact form
More information

description:
Rental Assistance
Over the past 20 years, Ramsey County has provided nearly $75 million in funding for the construction of 2,000 affordable housing rental units. All of the 992 units directly assisted with Ramsey County resources are affordable for tenants whose incomes are less than 80 percent of the area median income.

name: Homeless Prevention Assistance
description:
Homeless Prevention Assistance
If you or your family are seeking emergency shelter please visit the
Housing Services and Support page
for assistance.
There are several short-term financial assistance resources available to Ramsey County residents in order to prevent or resolve homelessness due to past due rent, mortgage and/or utilities. Residents may apply through Ramsey County and/or find a local community program to assist them with a household crisis.
Eligibility
Must be a Ramsey County resident.
Must have income that is at or below 200% of the
Federal Poverty Guidelines
.
At risk of homelessness or currently homeless.
How to apply
Through Ramsey County
Through a community provider
The ability to assist you or your household depends on meeting program eligibility criteria and funding available.
View a full list of homeless prevention resources
Housing Court Summons for eviction
If you have an upcoming court appearance for eviction, community organizations will be present on your court date to see if you qualify for financial help to assist you. Please be sure to request the “financial clinic” when you arrive.
Additional resources
You can find other short-term financial assistance resources through Minnesota's Family Homeless Prevention Assistance Program. Visit the
Minnesota Housing page
for more information.
Contact us
Housing Stability
651-266-1040
(Department staff)
651-266-1050
(Shelter Entry & Diversion staff)
Department information
Contact form

name: Justice-Impacted Resources
description:
Justice-Impacted Resources
Find resources and information below for help with an arrest, citation, drug/alcohol use or mental health concerns for adults and juveniles.

The
Mental Health & Crisis page
provides information about county-provided mental health and chemical health services.
FastTrackerMN.org
provides general information and real-time availability of mental and chemical services in the metro area.
If you have had contact with law enforcement
Contact the Second Judicial District
for the time and location of a court appearance, information related to charges, who your charging agency is, how to get an interpreter, or for any
other court-related questions
.
For felonies in Ramsey County, contact the
Ramsey County Attorney’s Office
.
For non-felonies in Saint Paul, contact the
Saint Paul City Attorney’s Office
.
Find an attorney
An attorney will be assigned to anyone who has been arrested and/or charged. However, if you’d like to speak to someone before a scheduled court date and do not yet have an attorney, you can reach out to either agency below.
The State Public Defender’s Office
The Neighborhood Justice Center

name: Housing Stability
description:
Housing Stability
Ramsey County Housing Stability administers and coordinates emergency supportive services to individuals and families experiencing sheltered and unsheltered homelessness, or housing instability. These efforts ensure that people’s basic needs are met while working toward long-term housing stability. In partnership with key community partners, Housing Stability works to reduce barriers, eliminate racial disparities and maximize resources that create pathways to long-term, affordable housing
Contact Housing Stability
Housing services & support
Winter Warming Spaces
Goals
Align all resources along the housing continuum for eviction prevention, unsheltered and sheltered homelessness and supportive housing in one department.
Align housing efforts with our
Economic Competitiveness & Inclusion Plan
.
Eliminate racial disparities in homelessness and housing instability.
Serve as a collaborative partner with community members, planners and other housing service providers.
Key initiatives
Since the closure of the Ramsey County shelters that were stood up during the COVID-19 pandemic, Ramsey County has continued to work closely with a network of government, nonprofit, public safety, business and other community partners to address the crisis of unsheltered homelessness in our County. Key department initiatives include:
The operation of
Winter Warming Spaces
to provide an indoor place for unsheltered individuals, youth, and families to spend the night and transportation to and from these warming spaces.
The creation of a
Shelter Entry & Diversion
team to provide a single point of contact and navigation to the right resources for anyone experiencing homelessness in Ramsey County, provide diversion services to single adults to prevent unnecessary entries into homelessness, and provide shelter reservations to single adults who need them for the coming night.
Navigation support and coordination of critical eviction or homeless prevention resources for people in Housing Court or at risk of eviction or housing instability.
Site-based navigation and service coordination functions throughout the community including at the Saint Paul Opportunity Center, the Family Service Center, youth drop-in spaces and at Saint Paul and suburban schools.
Operations team
Provides navigational support for people experiencing homelessness to access financial resources including Emergency Assistance, General Assistance, SNAP food stamps, the Minnesota Family Investment Program, housing resources through our Coordinated Entry System and Medicaid.

Shelter Entry & Diversion

Extra support and assistance programs are available for residents who meet eligibility guidelines.

Four-month program for Minnesota low-income families.

Available to individuals who have lost their jobs through no fault of their own.

An American Rescue Plan Act funded workforce initiative in partnership with the city of Saint Paul to help young workers aged 18-24 obtain their driver’s license as a needed work credential.

An American Rescue Plan Act funded workforce program offering a variety of new and expanded services to increase the number of quality child care programs in Ramsey County.

Everyone who receives cash assistance through the Minnesota Family Investment Program (MFIP) works with an employment counselors to support their career and family goals.

SNAP recipients have the opportunity to work with an employment guidance counselor to support their job search and to access to many other employment and training services.

WIOA Adult Program
Employment and training services to eligible adults 18 and over who are looking to gain skills and employment in healthcare, manufacturing, construction, administration and IT.
Young Adult Program
Provides employment and training opportunities to low-income and/or at-risk young adults.

description:
Jobs
Ramsey County employs nearly 4,000 people in full and part-time positions in a variety of fields. We welcome all applicants — you do not need to be a county resident to apply.
Job openings
View all current job openings

Notify me about job openings
Career training programs

Young Adult Career Academy Internship
Hiring process
How to apply
Go to the list of current positions available in our jobs portal.
Click on the job title from the list.
Select ‘apply’.
First time users set up a username and password and complete a profile. Be sure to save this login information for future access.
Additional information
Ramsey County sends all notices during the screening and examination process via email only.
All applications must be received by the posted closing date and time.
Positions with a continuous posting close date can close without notice.
Applicants must follow the specific instructions for each job posting. This may include uploading your application materials and entering data into the jobs portal. Incomplete applications may be rejected.
Include relevant education and experience.
The most recent application will be used if an applicant has applied multiple times for the same position.
A criminal background check is conducted after a conditional offer is made and accepted.
You can check your application status by logging into our jobs portal.
If you need assistance with the application process, please contact Human Resources.
A self-service computer is available to complete an application in the Human Resources office, located in
Metro Square
.
For technical support, contact
governmentjobs.com
or call
1-855-524-5627
Monday through Friday 8 a.m. - 8 p.m.
Virtual interviews
Microsoft Teams or other video conferencing software or phones may be used to conduct job interviews.
Veterans' preference
Ramsey County awards veterans' preference points to qualified veterans and spouses of deceased or disabled veterans in accordance with
Minnesota State Statute 197.447
.
Eligibility
You must be a United States citizen or a resident alien.
You have separated under honorable conditions from any branch of the armed forces for the United States.
You have service on active duty for 181 consecutive dates or by reason of disability incurred while on active duty, or for a full period ordered to active duty, or have separated by reason of disability incurred while service on active duty.
You are the spouse of a deceased or disabled veteran.
Veterans must:
Complete an application form for the position you are applying for.
Provide a legible copy of a DD214 Discharge Form.
Provide a legible copy of Form FL-802 or equivalent letter from Veterans Administration, if you are claiming disabled status.
All forms must be submitted by the job closing date via mail, hand delivery or fax:

name: Internships
description:
Internships
Ramsey County offers a variety of internship opportunities for students to enhance their academic studies with real world experience. Through internship placements, students receive relevant training while providing valuable public service to the community.
To qualify for an internship, candidates must be enrolled in a post-secondary, graduate-level or practicum program in coursework relevant to the duties of the internship.
Internships may be part-time or full-time. Some college programs require a minimum amount of hours in order for a student to earn college credits for their internship. Candidates should discuss these requirements with their supervisor prior to starting.
Internships may be paid or unpaid none offer benefits. Work locations vary by position.

name: Finance Career Fellowship
description:
Finance Career Fellowship
The Finance Professionals Career Fellowship Program is a Ramsey County initiative targeted at new, talented graduates with an interest in public service and the public finance profession.
Overview
Graduates accepted into the program will be appointed for a limited term of up to four years as an Accountant I or II and will serve 12-month rotational assignments in multiple departments. Candidates will gain exposure to county leaders and organization-wide perspectives through a structured quarterly cohort meeting with the Chief Financial Officer and senior county management.
After completion of this program, participants will have a full, well-rounded work experience that is intended to prepare them to compete favorably for available financial positions at Ramsey County through an open, competitive selection process
Participants will:
Agree to participate in the program for up to four years.
Agree to rotation assignments as determined by the program sponsor group.
Receive compensation and health benefits as made available to other employees in the same accountant classifications.
Be placed into unclassified, limited duration appointments that will not exceed four years for any one person, will not accrue seniority benefits and have no guarantee of future employment at Ramsey County.
Receive credit for their years of experience in the program that will be fully counted towards applicable work experience and minimum requirements in other open financial positions at Ramsey County.
Eligibility
Applicants to the program can be finishing up their last semester of school or have financial or accounting education and experience that equates to four years. Students who are in the process of graduating by summer 2023 are highly encouraged to apply.

Applications for the fellowship are currently being accepted.

name: Employee Compensation Manual
description:
Employee Compensation Manual
This document lists all job codes in the Ramsey County salary system, showing the following information for each job code:
Job code number
Job code description
Salary plan
Salary grade
Monthly salary range (minimum to maximum, rounded to the nearest whole dollar)
Some job titles include “*” in the title, indicating this job class is under review to be abolished as no longer needed. Some job codes have more than one salary plan or salary grade. This means that some positions in the job code are subject to different pay plans because of their bargaining unit status, or that employees in the job class are subject to different salary progression policies, or that employees in the job code are subject to different benefit plans.
How to use this manual
For quick reference: the monthly salary range (minimum and maximum rates) for each job code is listed in the job code index. To find this information, turn to the job code index, find the job code you are interested in, and read across to find the minimum and maximum salary rates for that job code, rounded to the nearest whole dollar.
For complete salary rate information: complete salary information for each job code (annual, monthly, biweekly, and hourly salary rates for each salary step in each salary range) is listed in the various salary plans. To find this information, turn to the job code index and find the job code, salary plan and salary grade. Then turn to the appropriate salary plan and find the salary.

name: Adult Protection
description:
Adult Protection
A vulnerable adult is any person 18 years of age or older who receives services from a licensed health care facility, or home care or personal care assistance provider in Minnesota, or who requires assistance with his or her own care. A vulnerable adult is also unable or unlikely to report mistreatment because of a physical or mental limitation.
A vulnerable adult is a victim of maltreatment when she or he is subjected to abuse, neglect or financial exploitation.
Maltreatment reports are referred to Ramsey County adult protection, the state office of health facility complaints, the Minnesota Department of Human Services, or law enforcement, depending on where the maltreatment occurred and whether criminal activity is suspected.

Report suspected child maltreatment
You can report suspected child maltreatment 24 hours a day, seven days a week. Call even if you are not sure whether or not to make a report, and a child protection social worker will assist you.

Mandated reporters
Must report suspected child maltreatment over the phone within 24 hours.
Must submit a written report within 72 hours.
Ramsey County investigates and assesses situations when a child's health or welfare may be at risk due to abuse, neglect or maltreatment. Child Protective Services may be provided to assure the safety of the child.
If out-of-home placement is necessary, due to significant and ongoing safety issues, work will continue with the parents toward returning the child home as soon as it is safe to do so. In cases when a safe return to the parents' home is not possible, alternative, permanent options are identified, which could include transfer of legal custody or adoption.
Special case child protection assessments
Ramsey County assess child abuse and neglect cases for victims and perpetrators in vulnerable populations, understanding that sometimes perpetrators are victims themselves or that vulnerable populations might need special attention and services.
Juvenile perpetrators
: Callers who report children or juveniles who commit non-family sexual offenses will be encouraged to contact the parents of the perpetrator and the police. If there is reason to believe a perpetrator is a sexual abuse victim, Ramsey County would conduct an abuse assessment.
Minor moms
: If a mother under the age of 18 is reported to have committed child abuse, the Minor Moms program will contact the new mother and conduct an assessment. If the mom is under 16, the county will consider assessing the incident as a neglect case.
Children with mental health and severe emotional disabilities
: We coordinate services for children with severe emotional disabilities who need protection.

Ramsey County child protective services
The Ramsey County Child Protection Division provides the following services:
Process intake for requests by counties and states who have determined that a parent who moves to Ramsey County is suspected of maltreatment. Ramsey County requires supporting documentation from a referral source.
Conduct interviews for other counties and states which are not part of the Metro County Agreement area, when a victim moves to Ramsey County prior to an assessment being completed. If a criminal investigation needs to be completed, the request is sent to appropriate police department.
Conduct assessment referrals for Family or Domestic Abuse Court
Conduct assessment referrals of a child under 10, who violated statutes to the degree that if they were older, a delinquency petition would have been filed.
Refer victims for interstate relative or non-relative foster care.
Place children who need care while parent is in hospital/treatment, if no responsible relatives are available.
Coordinate financial assistance services for children ages 16-18 who are living independently.
Determine the relationship of non-adjudicated custodial dads or caretakers with questionable documentation of relationship in coordination with the Financial Assistance Services department.
Refer victims of non-familial sexual abuse to counseling resources.
Manage adoption subsidy requests for changes in adoption subsidy.
Conduct home studies of parents when another state has custody and requests home study through interstate compact.

description:
Sexual Violence Prevention & Response
Sexual violence is any unwanted sexual attention (physical or non-physical contact) with someone who does not, or cannot consent (give permission). Sexual violence can happen to anyone regardless of age, income appearance or lifestyle. No matter what the circumstances, sexual violence is never the victim's fault.
If you are concerned about the immediate safety of you or someone you know, call 911.

name: Child Care Assistance
description:
Child Care Assistance
Ramsey County provides financial assistance through the Minnesota Child Care Assistance Program (CCAP) to parents who can’t afford child care. CCAP provides child care assistance to families who have been on the
Minnesota Family Investment Program
or
Diversionary Work Program
in the last 12 months and to others who meet income guidelines on a sliding scale. The Basic Sliding Fee (BSF) child care assistance program helps eligible families not receiving MFIP or DWP cash assistance pay for child care costs while parents participate in employment and/or approved education programs, or job search activities.
Funds are available for children up to age 12, and for children with special needs up to age 15.
Parents receiving child care assistance may pay part of the child care cost based on income and size of family. Learn more about income guidelines
here

name: Foster Care
description:
Foster Care
Ramsey County prioritizes keeping children safe with their families to avoid the trauma that often occurs when children are placed in out-of-home care. Our staff works to ensure children remain in their homes whenever possible by providing families with the resources they need to remain a healthy family unit.
When children are not able to stay safely in their homes, foster care staff place children in homes with extended family or kin whenever possible. More than 70% of new foster care applicants are relatives or kin.
Foster Care continues to license traditional, non-family/kin foster homes. We are looking for applicants who are:
Open to caring for older children, 8-17 years old.
Able to take sibling groups or children with medical needs of all ages.
Able to meet the needs of youth in terms of their race, ethnicity, religion, sexual orientation, language spoken etc.
Knowledgeable about trauma-informed care or willing to learn about the effect of trauma on children.
LGBTQ2S+ friendly.
New applicants for traditional foster homes who are seeking placement of children under the age of 8 will be prioritized for licensing at a later time.

name: Family Home Visiting
description:
Family Home Visiting
Home Visits are available in person with an option for virtual or phone visits when needed. Clients should call the Family Health Intake line at
651-266-1568
with questions.
Family home visiting is dedicated to the well-being of pregnant women, fathers and families with young children. Our home visiting services, and other programs, help families raise healthy children who are ready to succeed in school and in life. The staff are highly skilled and experienced in supporting families like yours. We respect all cultural backgrounds. We will support your relationship with your children and connect you with resources based on your family’s specific needs.
Family home visiting supports parents to give their baby the best start possible. A Public Health Nurse will visit you at home or another place you choose during your pregnancy and after your baby is born. Your nurse will offer helpful information and support as you develop new skills as a parent.
The importance of home visiting
All new parents can use some extra support.
A new baby means many changes for a family.
Babies learn so much in their first three years of life.
Parents are their baby’s first and most important teachers.
Parents want current information on how babies grow, change and develop.
We come to you.
Your nurse can help you
Care for yourself during pregnancy.
Prepare for the birth of your child.
Promote your baby’s health and well-being.
Know if your child is growing and developing well.
Create a safe home for your child.
Find healthcare, childcare, school/job options and other helpful services in the community.
Reach goals you want in your life.
Get started
If you are interested in a home visit from a Public Health nurse, there are a variety of ways to get started:
Call us at
651-266-1568
.
Ask your doctor to let us know.
If you go to WIC, the WIC staff can refer you.
Complete the
Family Health Services Request
form.

name: Parent Support Outreach Program
description:
Parent Support Outreach Program
Parent Support Outreach Program’s (PSOP) goal is to enhance family health and well-being, expand their support systems, and strengthen parent/child relationships. PSOP connects families to resources that provide safety and security for their children. Families can refer themselves, or can be referred by community members or social services agencies.
We help parents find support and handle many concerns together, including:
Your family’s health
Medical needs.
Mental health and crisis counseling.
Minimizing or eliminating substance use.
Child development/parenting skills.
Basic needs
Food and clothing.
Housing resources.
Safety from violence at home.
Academic success.

name: Sexual & Reproductive Health
description:
Sexual & Reproductive Health
WE'RE MOVING!
Clinic 555 is moving to 1850 Beam Ave. this spring/early summer.
Learn more
.
Small and personal sexual and reproductive health clinic located in downtown Saint Paul.
Clinic 555 offers sexual and reproductive health services for adults and teens. Services range from low cost to free. Fees are determined by a sliding fee scale based on family size and income. We accept public insurance and some private insurances. No one will be turned away for lack of funds. If available, please bring photo ID and insurance card.

name: Child and Teen Checkups
description:
Child and Teen Checkups
A Child and Teen Checkups (C&TC) is a full wellness checkup. It is available for any person who is under the age of 21 and eligible for Medical Assistance.
Health check-ups
It is recommended that children have check-ups at the following ages.
2 weeks.
2, 4 and 6 months.
9, 12 and 15 months.
18, 24 and 30 months.
Every year from age 3 through 20 years.
Dental check-ups
It is recommended to have dental check-ups at the following ages.
At first tooth or 12 months old.
Then every 6 months (2 times per year).

name: Young Adult Employment Program
description:
Young Adult Employment Program
The young adult program provides employment and training opportunities to low-income and/or at-risk young adults. Services provided by vendors include career planning, job search assistance, paid work experience and assistance with tuition costs.
Eligibility
The young adult program is open to individuals who:
Are 14-24 years old.
Live in Ramsey County.
Have a challenge to overcome to complete their education and/or obtain employment.
Services
Explore your future career opportunities.
Learn about potential employment trainings.
Experience work in a setting of your choice while getting paid.
Job search help (resumes, interviews, networking, etc.).
Earn your High School Diploma or GED.
Attend free workshops.
Plan out your educational pathway.
Vendors
Change Inc.
Change Inc. provides community-based education and training to young people who have dropped out, at-risk of dropping out or are immigrants and refugees. Change Inc.’s programming includes GAP school, education and enrichment, youth employment and training (including internships and work experiences) and social-emotional supports.
To connect with services and for additional information, contact:
Regina Edmisten,
[email protected]
Face to Face Health & Counseling Services, Inc.
Face to Face provides a variety of supports and services for youth and young adults who have/are experiencing homelessness. Face to Face Career Services will provide individualized career coaching, internships, job shadowing, tutoring & educational supports, youth leadership opportunities and workshops to teach professionalism, entrepreneurial skills and financial literacy.
To connect with services and for additional information, contact:
Matt Sexton,
[email protected]
Goodwill-Easter Seals of Minnesota - YouthBuild
Goodwill-Easter Seals provides training and career supports to young people interested in the construction/skilled-trades sector. The programming will include a formal construction training course that will provide participants with both classroom and hands-on instructions, and with paid work experience. Additionally, young people will receive customized career counseling and supports to ensure their success in the training program.
To connect with services and for additional information, contact:
Kristen Hoyles,
[email protected]
Hired -
Hired provides individualized career coaching, strategic support services, career readiness and digital and financial skill building. Youth will gain valuable experience through paid internships, growing professional & personal networks, increasing job and leadership skills, and engage in on-the-job experience to successfully launch into high demand careers.
To connect with services and for additional information, contact:
Rachel Grosskurth,
[email protected]

Tree Trust - Summer Youth Employment Program
Tree Trust provides summer employment and support opportunities to young people interested in exploring the green industry and other skilled trade careers. Participants will receive paid, hand-on experience working on outdoor construction, landscape projects or in local business internships. Additionally, Tree Trust provides mentorship and other workplace skills that are transferable to other industries and areas.

Urban Boatbuilders
Urban Boatbuilders provides youth and young adults with supports to help them gain technical, social-emotional and career readiness skills that will prepare them to pursue their academic and career goals. This includes career coaching and mentorship, paid employment, and hands-on, experiential learning through woodworking (building a boat). name: Service Center Navigators Referral Form
description:
Service Center Navigators Referral Form
The Ramsey County Service Centers Navigator referral allows for anyone to request assistance accessing services needed in the following areas: financial assistance services, social services, employment assistance and property tax. Please fill out the form below to request a navigator assistance consultation or to refer another resident. You may contact the Service Center Navigators by calling
651-266-0600
.
After submitting a referral, a Service Center Navigator will be in contact within two business days to assist you in accessing the requested services.
